caveats: a: The occupation source covers all NAICS 311600 and is employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted.
a: EPA's process description establishes mechanization but does not measure AI exposure or current software adoption.
rho: Continuous process equipment does not imply modern data infrastructure.
rho: The range combines relatively easy digital workflows with much harder physical and control-system changes.
e: The independent-versus-integrated count is from 1992 and is establishment-based, not firm-based.
e: Independent firms may still earn primarily commodity product revenue rather than explicit service fees.
s5: Only 30 LMM-band firms are estimated, so a few transfers materially change the rate.
s5: Plant acquisitions and route purchases are not necessarily qualifying firm-control transfers.
q: Revenue can sit on either side of the feedstock transaction depending on material value and local disposal alternatives.
q: Biofuel and feed prices can swamp the commercial effect of operating savings.
d5: The strongest demand evidence is historical growth, not a five-year forecast.
d5: Rendered inputs compete with vegetable oils, imported used cooking oil, and other feed proteins.
o: Alternative disposal and processing methods can replace the eligible operator without replacing demand for byproduct management.
o: Integrated rendering would preserve physical demand but move it outside the lens.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring route-based collection and byproduct handling for external generators, often economically supported by sale of recovered fats, protein meals, and grease and sometimes bundled with grease-trap or wastewater services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The lens is narrowed to independent external-source rendering because the code combines recurring outsourced collection/processors with captive integrated plants; their eligibility and value-capture mechanics are materially different.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive rendering within integrated meatpacking plants, firms principally processing only internally generated material, pure commodity traders, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market independent renderers that repeatedly collect and process animal byproducts, mortalities, meat scraps, or grease from external slaughterhouses, food processors, farms, retailers, restaurants, and institutions.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for broader NAICS 311600, not wage-weighted task exposure in 311613. EPA process descriptions show conveyorized, pumped, filtered, and continuous-cooker workflows, and the estimate maps AI-enabled routing, sensing, process optimization, predictive maintenance, inspection, and administration onto the broader occupation mix. It may overstate exposure where hands-on cleaning and maintenance dominate and understate it where collection routing is labor-intensive.
caveats: The occupation source covers all NAICS 311600 and is employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted.
EPA's process description establishes mechanization but does not measure AI exposure or current software adoption.
a.change_evidence: A 311613 payroll-by-occupation file joined to time-and-motion studies for collection routes, receiving, cooker control, laboratory work, maintenance, wastewater, sales, and compliance, with task-level vendor automation benchmarks.
a.rationale: Broader animal slaughtering and processing employment is 59.77% production, 18.43% transportation/material moving, 6.47% maintenance, and 4.31% office support. Rendering is already mechanized, so the incremental AI opportunity is concentrated in route dispatch, feedstock classification, process-control decisions, quality monitoring, predictive maintenance, records, and exception handling rather than replacing the core thermal process.

d5.bridge: USDA measures recent biomass-diesel feedstock use and rendered-protein use, not constant-price demand for independent rendering service over exactly five years. The range translates continued meat byproduct generation plus expanded outlets for fats and proteins into processing demand; it may overstate demand if policy-supported biofuel use reverses or imports displace domestic material and understate it if more generators outsource captive rendering.
caveats: The strongest demand evidence is historical growth, not a five-year forecast.
Rendered inputs compete with vegetable oils, imported used cooking oil, and other feed proteins.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year plant-level forecasts for domestic slaughter byproduct tonnage, restaurant grease collection, renewable-fuel obligations and credits, animal-fat imports, feed use, and announced renewable-diesel capacity, translated to eligible independent-processor volumes.
d5.rationale: Raw-material volume is anchored to slaughter, food processing, retail, and restaurant activity, while recovered fats and proteins have feed and energy uses. Biomass-based diesel output rose from 1.8 billion gallons in 2016 to 4.6 billion in 2023, with almost 12 billion pounds of animal fats and greases used in 2023; this supports demand but also exposes the outlook to policy, substitution, imports, and commodity cycles.

e.bridge: EPA directly distinguishes independent plants collecting from varied offsite sources from integrated plants, but its only plant split is a 1992 estimate of 150 independent and 100 integrated plants, not the current LMM firm share. The range uses that historical structure and current descriptions of independent collection activities; consolidation and ownership changes could move the true share in either direction.
caveats: The independent-versus-integrated count is from 1992 and is establishment-based, not firm-based.
Independent firms may still earn primarily commodity product revenue rather than explicit service fees.
e.change_evidence: A current census matching EPA and state permits, USDA feed registrations, fleet depots, and corporate ownership for all estimated LMM firms, coding offsite third-party feedstock volume and service revenue.
e.rationale: Independent renderers repeatedly serve external generators and fit the lens, while captive integrated plants do not. EPA's historical characterization places independent plants at 60% of the combined plant count, consistent with a central estimate slightly above half after allowing for consolidation and multi-plant firms.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311613 ($634,811,984) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311613 ($8,197,827,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $732,210 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311613); class <5: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,511,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $382,928 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,039,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,683,459 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,235,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $950,914 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,754,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,232,521 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $106,605,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,257,364 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $592,096,909 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $90,294,779 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 30

o.bridge: EPA and USDA describe physical collection, rapid processing, pathogen control, traceability, separation, drying, and storage, but do not measure the future operator-required share. The estimate treats custody, hauling, thermal processing, emissions and wastewater control, and accountable product release as services that software cannot supply alone; it could be high if generators adopt on-site alternatives or integrated plants internalize more work.
caveats: Alternative disposal and processing methods can replace the eligible operator without replacing demand for byproduct management.
Integrated rendering would preserve physical demand but move it outside the lens.
o.change_evidence: Generator-level studies of on-site rendering, anaerobic digestion, landfill, composting, and synthetic substitute adoption, plus regulatory approvals for unattended rendering and evidence on outsourcing versus reintegration by meat processors.
o.rationale: Perishable byproducts must be physically collected or received and converted through high-temperature, mechanical, and environmental-control systems. AI can automate decisions and controls, but the external generator still needs an accountable fleet-and-plant operator unless the material is eliminated, rendered on site, landfilled, digested, or otherwise diverted.

q.bridge: A public-company filing describes collection, grease services, and recovered-product sales, while USDA documents commodity end markets; neither measures five-year retention of implemented gross benefits in LMM independent renderers. The range infers sharing through feedstock rebates, collection prices, service bids, and commodity-product pricing. A large integrated company's model may overstate service breadth and bargaining power for LMM firms.
caveats: Revenue can sit on either side of the feedstock transaction depending on material value and local disposal alternatives.
Biofuel and feed prices can swamp the commercial effect of operating savings.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice panels showing generator fees or rebates, minimum-volume clauses, index formulas, route economics, and product realizations before and after automation, with local competitor bids and renewal outcomes.
q.rationale: Efficiency gains can initially accrue through denser routes, lower fuel and overtime, higher yields, less downtime, and better product quality. Over time, generators can demand higher payments or lower collection charges, service competitors can reprice, and recovered fats and proteins clear in commodity-linked markets, limiting retention despite local route density and permitting barriers.

caveats: Continuous process equipment does not imply modern data infrastructure.
The range combines relatively easy digital workflows with much harder physical and control-system changes.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level implementation cohorts from rendering control-system, fleet-telematics, machine-vision, and maintenance vendors showing quote-to-live conversion, integration cost, operator overrides, realized labor hours, uptime, and three-year persistence.
rho.rationale: Route optimization, document automation, camera-based receiving checks, process alarms, and predictive maintenance can be layered onto existing plants within five years. Integration with legacy controls, harsh operating conditions, feedstock variability, fleet hardware, environmental permitting, food/feed safety, small technical teams, and the cost of downtime prevent full implementation of the exposed opportunity.

caveats: Only 30 LMM-band firms are estimated, so a few transfers materially change the rate.
Plant acquisitions and route purchases are not necessarily qualifying firm-control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based history linking facility permits and USDA registrations to PitchBook/Capital IQ transactions, ownership changes, closures, and asset sales, supplemented by interviews with rendering executives and environmental-services deal advisers.
s5.rationale: The five-year qualifying-transfer probability is a bounded judgment. Scarce permitted infrastructure, route density, recurring feedstock relationships, and strategic buyer synergies can support sales; environmental liabilities, odor and wastewater constraints, commodity volatility, specialized plants, and family or corporate ownership can instead delay a sale, prompt closure, or produce an internal transfer.

principal_driver: Route-dense recurring collection and mission-critical physical conversion of unavoidable perishable byproducts.
principal_weakness: A small, consolidated firm universe and commodity-linked economics leave eligibility, transfer supply, and benefit retention poorly measured.

sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation mix: production 59.77%, transportation and material moving 18.43%, maintenance and repair 6.47%, and office support 4.31%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311600

sources.supports: EPA describes batch and continuous rendering, conveyors, cookers, presses, pumps, centrifuges, storage, feedstock variability, and process conditions.
sources.title: AP-42 Section 9.5.3: Meat Rendering Plants

sources.supports: EPA distinguishes integrated and independent renderers, lists offsite source types, and reports a 1992 estimate of 150 independent and 100 integrated U.S. plants.
sources.title: Final Background Document for Meat Rendering Plants, Section 9.5.3

sources.supports: USDA reports biomass-based diesel output rising from 1.8 billion gallons in 2016 to 4.6 billion in 2023 and almost 12 billion pounds of animal fats and greases used in 2023.
sources.title: Growing Biomass-Based Diesel Production Drives Demand for Animal Fats, Waste Oils, and Grease

sources.supports: USDA ARS reports 4.5 million tons of animal-derived protein products, about 85% used as animal-feed ingredients, and documents variation in product energy value.
sources.title: Energy and Nutrient Digestibility in Four Sources of Meat and Bone Meal and Animal Protein Blends Fed to Growing Pigs

sources.supports: Public-company filing describes external-source rendering, spent-cooking-oil collection, grease-trap services, supplier types, recovered products, traceability, rapid processing, and outsourcing by integrated meat processors.
sources.title: Darling International Investor Presentation

sources.supports: USDA reports 170 U.S. facilities processing more than 56 billion pounds of animal byproduct and describes source materials, output uses, process controls, and industry structure.
sources.title: After Action Report: Rendering Workshop

title: Rendering and Meat Byproduct Processing
